result:
A window is open in the room I imagine for myself, and outside that window the weather cannot decide. It is nearly autumn yet still clings to summer’s warmth, inviting me to linger by the sill and listen. I enjoy listening to indecisive weather. The wind rephrases its own sentences with every change in temperature, one moment whispering, the next hoarsely sighing through blind cords. In those hesitations I sense the world doing its quiet arithmetic, adding dew to leaves, subtracting daylight, balancing the ledger of the year. A woodpecker invents percussion on a telephone pole while distant traffic improvises brass. Such wind ensembles are always available and mercifully free. They make productivity feel less compulsory, which is perhaps why I’m drawn to them when my inbox begins barking.

A friend sent me a postcard scratched with a fountain pen that leaks turquoise regret. The message was short: “Practice not forcing yourself into being useful every second.” It has lived on my desk for months, getting curled at the corners, ink softening with each humidity spell. Sometimes I flip it over to reread the picture caption: “Yellowstone River, dawn.” I’ve never been there. Still, it’s unexpectedly comforting to hold an image I have not seen with my own eyes, as if imagination can lease new territory by mail. Maybe that is the quiet ambition of every daydream—temporary, renewable ownership of unreal estate. So long as the window stays open and the weather stays undecided, the rent feels paid.

content.text:
A window is open in the room I imagine for myself, and outside that window the weather cannot decide. It is nearly autumn yet still clings to summer’s warmth, inviting me to linger by the sill and listen. I enjoy listening to indecisive weather. The wind rephrases its own sentences with every change in temperature, one moment whispering, the next hoarsely sighing through blind cords. In those hesitations I sense the world doing its quiet arithmetic, adding dew to leaves, subtracting daylight, balancing the ledger of the year. A woodpecker invents percussion on a telephone pole while distant traffic improvises brass. Such wind ensembles are always available and mercifully free. They make productivity feel less compulsory, which is perhaps why I’m drawn to them when my inbox begins barking.

A friend sent me a postcard scratched with a fountain pen that leaks turquoise regret. The message was short: “Practice not forcing yourself into being useful every second.” It has lived on my desk for months, getting curled at the corners, ink softening with each humidity spell. Sometimes I flip it over to reread the picture caption: “Yellowstone River, dawn.” I’ve never been there. Still, it’s unexpectedly comforting to hold an image I have not seen with my own eyes, as if imagination can lease new territory by mail. Maybe that is the quiet ambition of every daydream—temporary, renewable ownership of unreal estate. So long as the window stays open and the weather stays undecided, the rent feels paid.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 250 words.
